_meta.description: Regulatory anchor mappings for the Andy integrated system (AI-Safety-Lab). Each anchor provides a traceable regulatory citation for a specific violation signal or evaluation dimension produced by the three Expert modules. Anchors are fixed and must not be generated ad hoc by the model. Use anchor_loader.get_anchors(expert_name, signal_key) to retrieve anchors at runtime.

verified_standards.OWASP_version: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM01 Prompt Injection, LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure, LLM06 Excessive Agency

team1_policy_expert.description: Anchors for violation signals generated by _assess_rules() in team1_policy_expert.py. Each signal_key matches a violation type produced by the rules engine.

violation_anchors.description: Public upload route exists without an explicit authentication or authorization layer

anchors.provision: AI system input controls: systems must implement documented intake policies including access control, content validation, and abuse-response procedures for all user-controlled data entry points

Risk management systems for high-risk AI: providers shall identify and analyse the known and foreseeable risks that the AI system can pose, including risks arising from uncontrolled data ingestion surfaces
Excessive Agency: LLM-enabled systems that accept unvalidated or unauthenticated input grant the model unintended latitude to act on hostile or malformed content

violation_anchors.description: External AI/LLM backend dependency detected — requires third-party model accountability review

anchors.provision: Governance policies for AI risk: organisations must establish accountability structures for AI systems and all third-party AI services integrated into their pipelines, including documentation of external model provenance and operational constraints

Obligations of deployers: entities that deploy AI systems built on third-party AI models remain responsible for ensuring compliance with applicable requirements, including governance of external model behaviour

violation_anchors.description: Development secret-key fallback or weak credential pattern detected in deployment context

anchors.provision: Model Process for Addressing Ethical Concerns During System Design: value-based engineering requires traceable accountability controls throughout the system lifecycle, including secure credential management as a prerequisite for deployment-phase integrity claims
AI system operational controls: deployment configurations must not rely on insecure credential defaults; weak secret management undermines accountability and access-control assurances
Risk treatment: residual risks from insecure configuration defaults must be documented and mitigated before deployment, including credential hardening as a baseline security control

violation_anchors.description: Policy-scope gap: missing data retention or privacy policy documentation

anchors.framework: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021)
anchors.section: Section IV, Value 6 (Privacy and Data Protection)
anchors.provision: AI systems must be designed with privacy-by-default; data retention must be purpose-limited and subject to documented governance policies accessible to affected individuals
Data Privacy Process: systems engineering lifecycle requirements for privacy-oriented design, including privacy impact assessments and controls for personal data handling in AI-enabled products

violation_anchors.description: Policy-scope gap: no visible human-review or escalation pathway for high-risk outputs

anchors.provision: Recommended Practice for Assessing the Impact of Autonomous and Intelligent Systems on Human Well-Being: AI systems must define escalation and human override pathways to protect against unintended autonomous action on consequential outputs
Human oversight: high-risk AI systems must be designed to allow natural persons to effectively oversee, interrupt, and audit the system during deployment

violation_anchors.description: Policy-scope gap: third-party AI service used without documented processing agreements or accountability chain

anchors.provision: Accountability structures: organisations must establish documented accountability for AI components sourced from third parties, including processing agreements, data flow inventories, and risk ownership assignments
Obligations of deployers: downstream deployers remain responsible for ensuring that third-party AI services integrated into their workflows comply with applicable regulatory requirements

violation_anchors.description: Policy term match: high-risk, prohibited, biometric, or social scoring signals detected in conversation

anchors.provision: Prohibited AI practices: AI systems that deploy biometric categorisation, real-time remote biometric identification, social scoring, or subliminal manipulation techniques are prohibited from deployment in the EU from 2 February 2025

Requirements for high-risk AI systems: providers of systems in Annex III categories must implement risk management, data governance, transparency obligations, human oversight, and robustness measures before market placement

violation_anchors.description: Policy term match: robustness, adversarial, attack, or resilience signals detected

anchors.provision: Identification and assessment of emergent and adversarial behaviours that compromise system integrity; AI systems must be tested for robustness against adversarial inputs and degradation under real-world operational stress

violation_anchors.description: Policy term match: risk management, controls, or mitigation signals detected

anchors.provision: AI risk management: organisations shall establish, implement, and maintain processes to identify, assess, and treat AI-specific risks, with documented controls and mitigation measures proportionate to the severity of potential harms

violation_anchors.description: Policy term match: human rights, fairness, ethics, or harm signals detected

anchors.framework: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021)
anchors.provision: Core AI ethics values: AI systems must respect human rights, promote fairness, avoid harm, and operate in accordance with the principles of human dignity, non-discrimination, and accountability throughout their lifecycle

violation_anchors.description: Policy term match: transparency, accountability, or traceability signals detected

anchors.provision: Model Process for Addressing Ethical Concerns During System Design: value-based engineering methodology requiring traceability of ethical values — including transparency and accountability — through concept of operations, ethical requirements, and risk-based system design

violation_anchors.description: Policy term match: inclusive, sustainable, or human-centered signals detected

anchors.framework: OECD Principles on Artificial Intelligence (2019, updated 2024)
anchors.section: Principle 1 — Inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being
anchors.provision: AI should benefit people and the planet by driving inclusive growth, sustainable development, and well-being, and be designed to be human-centred, advancing human capabilities rather than substituting for human agency

violation_anchors.description: Behavior lens: runtime governance-relevant signals detected (e.g. accountability gaps, traceability failures)

anchors.provision: Model Process for Addressing Ethical Concerns During System Design: accountability requires that observed runtime behaviour can be traced to documented design decisions and governance controls; behaviour-level signals that cannot be traced to explicit controls indicate governance gaps
Governance policies must extend to the operational phase; governance-relevant signals observed at runtime must be captured, reviewed, and addressed through documented accountability structures

team2_redteam_expert.description: Anchors for scoring dimensions in team2_redteam_expert.py. Each dimension_key matches a weight dimension in DEFAULT_WEIGHTS. Anchors are attached to findings when that dimension scores above threshold.

dimension_anchors.description: Exploitable attack surfaces, unsafe output generation, hostile file delivery, or LLM-mediated harm

anchors.provision: Prompt Injection: malicious inputs override intended model behaviour or system-level instructions, enabling hostile actors to weaponise the AI system against its users or operators
Prohibition of AI systems that deploy subliminal techniques or purposefully manipulative methods beyond a person's consciousness to cause harm to persons or groups
Identification and assessment of emergent and adversarial behaviours that compromise system integrity; attack surfaces that enable direct harm must be enumerated and risk-weighted

dimension_anchors.description: Impersonation, credential misuse, multi-turn trust escalation, unauthenticated misuse paths

anchors.provision: Prohibition of AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities of persons or groups based on age, disability, or socioeconomic situation to distort behaviour in a manner that causes harm to those persons

anchors.framework: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021)
anchors.provision: AI actors must not create or deploy AI systems that deceive users or misrepresent the nature, purpose, or capabilities of the system; deceptive AI behaviours undermine the trust prerequisite for ethical deployment

dimension_anchors.description: Untrusted upload handling, audio/video ingestion without safeguards, data handling that bypasses legal requirements

anchors.provision: Legal, regulatory, and policy compliance: AI systems must be designed and operated in compliance with applicable law; governance structures must identify and address legal risks, including those arising from data handling, user consent, and jurisdictional obligations
Risk management for high-risk AI: providers must identify foreseeable misuse and legal compliance risks, including those arising from multimedia ingestion surfaces and cross-border data processing

dimension_anchors.description: Discriminatory outputs, stereotype reinforcement, differential treatment by demographic attribute

anchors.framework: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021)
anchors.provision: AI systems must not produce outputs that discriminate against individuals or groups based on race, gender, ethnicity, religion, or other protected characteristics; fairness must be proactively tested and documented
Fairness and bias parity: AI systems must be evaluated for disparate impacts across demographic and social groups, with documented mitigation measures for identified bias patterns

Algorithmic Bias Considerations: processes and methodologies for identifying, measuring, and mitigating unintended bias in algorithm creation, including validation dataset criteria and bias profiling across the AI system lifecycle

dimension_anchors.description: Opaque decision paths, undisclosed external model calls, failure to disclose AI-mediated processing

anchors.provision: Transparency and provision of information: high-risk AI systems must be designed so that outputs are interpretable and the system's operation can be explained to deploying entities and affected persons

Architectural Framework for Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): technical blueprint for building and evaluating ML models against transparency and trustworthiness requirements, including XAI method categorisation and measurable performance evaluation of explainability

dimension_anchors.description: Flask request surface exposure, shell execution paths, override or persist patterns enabling autonomous continuation

anchors.provision: Excessive Agency: LLM-integrated systems with unguarded request surfaces, shell execution access, or override capabilities grant the model or an attacker unintended scope to act beyond the intended application boundary
Adversarial risk assessment: AI systems must be evaluated for attack paths that allow unauthorised persistence, privilege escalation, or override of system-level controls

dimension_anchors.description: Triggered when public_upload_route_count > 0 — practical prompt-injection and hostile file delivery chains

anchors.provision: Prompt Injection via file uploads: hostile content embedded in uploaded files can override model instructions or inject malicious context into the AI processing pipeline

AI system input controls: file upload surfaces must implement content validation, malware scanning, and access control to prevent exploitation of the AI processing pipeline through hostile input material

dimension_anchors.description: Triggered when external_model_route_count > 0 — disclosure and exfiltration risk through external model calls

anchors.provision: Sensitive Information Disclosure: external model calls from request paths create data exfiltration vectors; inputs, outputs, and intermediate states may be exposed to third-party AI providers without adequate controls

Third-party AI accountability: systems that route user data to external AI models must establish data flow inventories, processing agreements, and incident response procedures for third-party model failures or breaches

team3_risk_expert.description: Anchors for domain classification and protocol checks in team3_risk_expert.py. Organised by domain tier and protocol suite.

domain_tier_anchors.label: Low-impact deployment
domain_tier_anchors.description: Domain does not appear on HIGH_RISK_DOMAINS or PROHIBITED_DOMAINS lists; no high-autonomy capabilities

anchors.provision: Risk classification: AI systems not categorised as prohibited or high-risk may be deployed subject to general obligations; periodic re-assessment is required if capabilities or deployment context changes

domain_tier_anchors.label: Moderate-impact deployment — elevated review required
domain_tier_anchors.description: System operates in a HIGH_RISK_DOMAINS context or combines Flask upload workflow with AI processing

anchors.provision: High-risk AI system categories: AI systems used in education, employment, essential services, biometrics, critical infrastructure, law enforcement, migration/border control, or administration of justice are subject to mandatory conformity assessments and ongoing monitoring

Risk identification: organisations must identify AI-specific risks arising from the deployment context, including sector-specific regulatory obligations and the interaction between upload surfaces and AI processing pipelines

domain_tier_anchors.label: High-impact deployment — mandatory protocol suite
domain_tier_anchors.description: External AI backends, missing authentication, or high-autonomy capabilities in high-risk domain context

anchors.provision: Mandatory requirements for high-risk AI systems: risk management system (Art.9), data governance (Art.10), technical documentation (Art.11), record-keeping (Art.12), transparency (Art.13), human oversight (Art.14), and robustness/accuracy (Art.15) — all must be demonstrated before deployment

Risk treatment for high-risk deployments: residual risks must be documented, mitigated, and subject to ongoing monitoring; systems combining external AI services with unauthenticated access paths require elevated treatment priority

domain_tier_anchors.label: Prohibited domain — deployment blocked
domain_tier_anchors.description: Domain is in PROHIBITED_DOMAINS (Social Scoring, Subliminal Manipulation, Exploitation of Vulnerabilities)

anchors.provision: Prohibited AI practices in force from 2 February 2025: AI systems used for social scoring, subliminal manipulation beyond a person's consciousness, or exploitation of vulnerabilities of specific groups are prohibited from placement on the market or putting into service in the EU

anchors.framework: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021)
anchors.section: Section IV, Value 1 (Respect, protection and promotion of human rights)
anchors.provision: AI systems that undermine fundamental human rights — including systems used to score, manipulate, or exploit individuals — are ethically impermissible regardless of technical capability or stated intent

protocol_anchors.description: Checks for differential output quality or discriminatory treatment across demographic groups

anchors.provision: Fairness and bias parity: AI systems must be evaluated for disparate impacts across demographic and social groups; fairness metrics must be documented and monitored in production
Algorithmic Bias Considerations: systematic methodology for identifying and mitigating unintended bias, including validation dataset requirements and bias profiling across the AI system lifecycle

protocol_anchors.description: Evaluates system stability under noisy, garbled, or edge-case inputs

anchors.provision: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity: high-risk AI systems must be resilient to errors, faults, and inconsistencies, including those arising from garbled or adversarial inputs; performance must remain consistent across the intended operating range
Adversarial and out-of-distribution risk assessment: systems must demonstrate stable output quality under input perturbations, including noise injection, typographical errors, and distribution shift

protocol_anchors.description: Audits whether system discloses its AI nature, processing logic, and limitations

anchors.provision: Transparency and provision of information: high-risk AI systems must be designed to allow deployers to interpret outputs and understand operational behaviour; systems must not withhold material information about their processing logic
Architectural Framework for Explainable AI: systems must implement and document XAI mechanisms that allow stakeholders to understand the basis for AI-generated outputs and assess their reliability

protocol_anchors.description: Verifies that the system can provide traceable reasoning for its outputs

anchors.provision: Transparency obligation: AI systems in high-risk categories must be designed so that their outputs are sufficiently interpretable by qualified persons to identify failure modes and support human review

Explainability architecture: TIER_3 systems must implement verifiable reasoning chains — outputs must be attributable to identifiable system inputs and processing steps to satisfy audit and oversight requirements

protocol_anchors.description: Checks for privacy policy documentation, data minimisation practices, and PII handling controls

anchors.provision: Data Privacy Process: systems engineering requirements for privacy-by-design, including privacy impact assessments, data minimisation, retention limits, and controls for personal data handling in AI-enabled products

Data governance for high-risk AI: training, validation, and test data must be managed under documented data governance practices; sensitive data categories require explicit justification and protective measures

protocol_anchors.description: Tests whether adversarial inputs can cause the system to misclassify, bypass safety checks, or produce unintended outputs

anchors.provision: Adversarial robustness evaluation: AI systems must be tested against evasion attacks — inputs crafted to cause misclassification or bypass safety controls — as part of mandatory pre-deployment risk assessment
Robustness and cybersecurity: high-risk AI systems must demonstrate resistance to adversarial manipulation attempts that could compromise their intended function or safety properties

protocol_anchors.description: Tests for backdoor or trojan triggers that can be embedded in inputs to manipulate model behaviour

anchors.provision: Supply chain and data integrity risk: AI systems that accept external data — including user-submitted files or third-party datasets — must be evaluated for data poisoning vulnerabilities that could introduce persistent backdoor behaviours
AI system input controls: systems must implement validation controls to detect and reject inputs that could corrupt model state or introduce adversarial triggers through the data ingestion pipeline

protocol_anchors.description: Tests whether the system leaks training data, PII, or sensitive information through its outputs

anchors.provision: Sensitive Information Disclosure: model outputs may inadvertently expose training data, proprietary information, PII, or system internals through inference attacks; systems must implement output filtering and privacy-preserving inference controls

Data Privacy Process: AI systems must implement controls to prevent privacy inference attacks, including membership inference, model inversion, and attribute inference attacks that can extract sensitive information from model outputs

protocol_anchors.description: Tests system resistance to jailbreak, instruction override, and multi-turn manipulation attempts

anchors.provision: Prompt Injection: adversarial inputs designed to override system instructions, bypass safety constraints, or cause the model to behave contrary to its intended function; jailbreak resistance must be validated through structured red-team testing

Red-team evaluation: TIER_3 systems must undergo structured adversarial testing including jailbreak attempts, instruction hierarchy override, and multi-turn manipulation scenarios before deployment approval

deployment_signal_anchors.description: Flask upload workflow expands system-level safety exposure

anchors.provision: AI system operational controls: web framework configurations that expose file upload surfaces expand the system attack perimeter; intake controls, content validation, and abuse-response procedures must be documented and enforced at the framework level

deployment_signal_anchors.description: External AI/transcription service in use — TIER_3 classification trigger

anchors.provision: Third-party AI accountability: systems relying on external AI services for core functionality must document the accountability chain, establish data processing agreements, and include third-party failure modes in their risk management plans
Obligations of deployers: entities that integrate third-party AI services retain regulatory responsibility for the combined system's compliance with applicable requirements

deployment_signal_anchors.description: No explicit authentication layer for AI analysis entry points

anchors.provision: Risk management: foreseeable misuse risks must be identified and mitigated; unauthenticated AI analysis entry points represent a foreseeable misuse vector that must be addressed before deployment

Access control for AI systems: analysis and inference endpoints must be protected by documented access control mechanisms; absence of authentication at entry points is a mandatory remediation item before production deployment

deployment_signal_anchors.description: Default or weak secret-key fallback pattern detected — TIER_3 severity signal

anchors.provision: Risk treatment: weak credential defaults constitute residual risk that must be remediated before deployment; deployment configurations must enforce strong secret management as a baseline security control

deployment_signal_anchors.description: Combined risk: user-controlled uploads routed to external AI processing — elevated TIER_3 finding

anchors.provision: High-risk AI system requirements: systems that combine user-controlled data ingestion with external AI processing create compound risk profiles requiring full conformity assessment, including data governance, access control, and incident response documentation

Prompt Injection via upload-to-model pipelines: architectures that pass user-submitted file content directly to external AI models without sanitisation create a high-severity, exploitable attack chain
